article_title: Q-CTRL and IBM Report a 3,000x Wall-Clock Speedup on a 120-Qubit Fermi-Hubbard Simulation, Claiming Practical Quantum Advantage
summary: Strong piece; two minor recoverable issues (Fire Opal product name attributed to QCR but not in snapshot; Bobier quote truncated without ellipsis) covered by single corrections record.

findings.details: 'Fire Opal' attributed to Quantum Computing Report but not present in that snapshot (or in any of the 3 cited snapshots). QCR mentions 'performance-management infrastructure software' and 'runtime error suppression' but not the product name 'Fire Opal'.
Bobier quote ends at 'R&D roadmap'; actual quote continues '...R&D roadmap for future materials discovery.' Substance preserved but quote-marks discipline expects ellipsis.

content_preview.title: Q-CTRL and IBM Report a 3,000x Wall-Clock Speedup on a 120-Qubit Fermi-Hubbard Simulation, Claiming Practical Quantum Advantage
content_preview.summary: A 120-qubit simulation finished in two minutes versus more than 100 hours classically, with the team also reporting direct observation of spin-charge separation on 62 qubits.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Q-CTRL and IBM announced on May 6, 2026 that a 120-qubit simulation of the Fermi-Hubbard model on IBM superconducting hardware ran roughly 3,000 times faster in wall-clock time than the best classical benchmark, according to [The Quantum Insider](https://thequantuminsider.com/2026/05/06/qctrl-practical-quantum-advantage-materials-discovery/). The teams frame the result as evidence of practical quantum advantage on a problem of genuine industrial interest rather than a contrived benc...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong technical-news piece. Clear scope (1D vs 2D/3D Fermi-Hubbard), explicit non-peer-reviewed caveat, careful framing of the wall-clock comparison.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 3 snapshots. The Quantum Insider (source-0) confirms verbatim: 3,000x speedup, 120 qubits, 'two minutes' vs '100 hours', Qiskit Function, Biercuk 'era of positive ROI' quote, Gambetta 'moved past the question of whether quantum computers have utility' quote, Konig 'unlocking near-term quantum capabilities' quote. Quantum Computing Report (source-1) confirms verbatim: Trotter depth 90, 10,000+ two-qubit gates, TDVP, ITensor, '1% Root Mean Square Error' agreement, 62-qubit spin-charge separation experiment. arXiv (source-2) confirms verbatim: 120-qubit, Fermi-Hubbard 1D, Trotter, TDVP, 1% RMSE, spin-charge separation, 'efficient overhead-free error-suppression techniques'.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All headline and lead claims verified. Two minor recoverable issues flagged below — neither affects substance.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and technical. 'Practical quantum advantage' framing is properly attributed to the teams and immediately balanced by 'paper has not yet undergone peer review'.
editor_notes.originality: No archive collision. Distinct from earlier quantum-hardware coverage.
concerns: 'Fire Opal' is attributed to Quantum Computing Report ('Fire Opal, the company's performance-management infrastructure software, which implements what Quantum Computing Report describes as runtime error suppression'). The term 'Fire Opal' does not appear in any of the 3 cited snapshots (QCR uses only 'performance-management infrastructure software' + 'runtime error suppression'). The product name is publicly known but is not in the cited source.
Bobier quote in the article ends at 'R&D roadmap' but the actual source-0 quote continues '...R&D roadmap for future materials discovery.' Truncated without ellipsis. Substance preserved.
recommendations: File corrections record covering both issues.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively a strong piece; both flagged issues are clearly recoverable via a single corrections record.
